summary: O'Neil argues that users of free services like Google and credit agencies like Experian fundamentally misunderstand their role: they are the product being sold, not the customer. This framing explains why Google finds Europe's 'right to be forgotten' laws unreasonable and why credit agencies can ignore consumer complaints with impunity. She uses the metaphor of cows at a meat farm to illustrate how absurd it seems to companies when their 'products' demand better treatment. The post connects this business model to real harm, showing how Experian's $4.8 billion revenue makes FTC fines of $25.7 million over 14 years essentially meaningless. The power imbalance between billion-dollar companies and individual consumers renders existing consumer protection laws toothless.
key_points: Google's actual customers are advertisers, not users—users provide data that makes advertising more valuable
Anti-trust analysis of Google should focus on advertiser choices, not whether users have alternatives to Gmail or Search
Credit agencies like Experian treat consumers as products, which explains why they ignore the Fair Credit Reporting Act
Regulatory fines are too small relative to company revenues to change behavior—millions in penalties versus billions in revenue
Consumer protection laws lack sufficient enforcement power when the scale mismatch between fines and profits is this extreme
money_quotes: As a gmail and Google Search user, you are not a customer of Google. You are the product.
It'd be like a meat farm where the cows go on strike and demand better food. Cows are the product, and they aren't supposed to complain.
Mere people, whose mortgage application is being denied because of mistakes, are the product, not the customer, and they are almost by definition unimportant.
It's all about the scale of the fines compare to the scale of the business.
Million versus billion. It seems like the cows don't have much leverage.
At worst we treat them better when our customers demand it, not when the cows do.
key_insight: When you are the product rather than the customer, consumer protection laws fail because companies have no economic incentive to treat you well—only paying customers matter.
